section.title: Sin, Confession, and the Redeemer
section.sectionRationale: This chapter diagnoses exactly why salvation seemed delayed, not because God's arm was too short, but because sin had built a wall between the people and their God. It moves through unflinching confession into one of Isaiah's most striking images, God Himself, seeing no one to intercede, putting on His own armor of righteousness and salvation to act alone.
section.thesis: Israel's sin, not any weakness in God, has separated her from His help, and when God finds no human intercessor able to bring justice, He Himself acts, arming Himself with righteousness and salvation to come as Redeemer to those in Jacob who turn from transgression.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the LORD's hand is not too short to save; a raw confession of sin's true shape; the LORD sees no one to intercede and acts Himself; and the covenant promise, My Spirit and My words will never depart from you.

verses.text: Surely the arm of the LORD is not too short to save, nor His ear too dull to hear.
But your iniquities have built barriers between you and your God, and your sins have hidden His face from you, so that He does not hear.
For your hands are stained with blood, and your fingers with iniquity; your lips have spoken lies, and your tongue mutters injustice.

units.label: They hatch the eggs of vipers
units.note: a raw confession of sin's shape

verses.text: No one calls for justice; no one pleads his case honestly. They rely on empty pleas; they tell lies; they conceive mischief and give birth to iniquity.
They hatch the eggs of vipers and weave a spider’s web. Whoever eats their eggs will die; crack one open, and a viper is hatched.
Their cobwebs cannot be made into clothing, and they cannot cover themselves with their works. Their deeds are sinful deeds, and acts of violence are in their hands.
Their feet run to evil; they are swift to shed innocent blood. Their thoughts are sinful thoughts; ruin and destruction lie in their wake.
The way of peace they have not known, and there is no justice in their tracks. They have turned them into crooked paths; no one who treads on them will know peace.
Therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not reach us. We hope for light, but there is darkness; for brightness, but we walk in gloom.
Like the blind, we feel our way along the wall, groping like those without eyes. We stumble at midday as in the twilight; among the vigorous we are like the dead.
We all growl like bears and moan like doves. We hope for justice, but find none, for salvation, but it is far from us.
For our transgressions are multiplied before You, and our sins testify against us. Our transgressions are indeed with us, and we know our iniquities:
rebelling and denying the LORD, turning away from our God, speaking oppression and revolt, conceiving and uttering lies from the heart.
So justice is turned away, and righteousness stands at a distance. For truth has stumbled in the public square, and honesty cannot enter.
Truth is missing, and whoever turns from evil becomes prey. The LORD looked and was displeased that there was no justice.

units.label: His own arm brought Him salvation
units.note: the armor of righteousness and salvation

verses.text: He saw that there was no man; He was amazed that there was no one to {{paga|intercede}}. So His own arm brought salvation, and His own righteousness sustained Him.
He put on righteousness like a breastplate, and the helmet of salvation on His head; He put on garments of vengeance and wrapped Himself in a cloak of zeal.
So He will repay according to their deeds: fury to His enemies, retribution to His foes, and recompense to the islands.
So shall they fear the name of the LORD where the sun sets, and His glory where it rises. For He will come like a raging flood, driven by the breath of the LORD.

units.label: The Redeemer will come to Zion
units.note: My Spirit, My words, will not depart

verses.text: “The {{goel|Redeemer}} will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from transgression, ” declares the LORD.

“As for Me, this is My covenant with them,” says the LORD. “My Spirit will not depart from you, and My words that I have put in your mouth will not depart from your mouth or from the mouths of your children and grandchildren, from now on and forevermore,” says the LORD.

paga.gloss: To intercede, to meet or intervene on behalf of another. God is amazed to find no one able to paga, intercede, so He acts Himself; the same root describes the LORD laying the iniquity of all upon the Servant in 53:6 and the Servant making intercession for transgressors in 53:12, tying this chapter's crisis directly to its answer.

goel.gloss: Redeemer, kinsman-deliverer, obligated by covenant bond to rescue and restore. Paul quotes this exact verse, the Deliverer will come from Zion, in Romans 11:26, applying it to the future salvation of Israel through Christ, the ultimate go'el who comes to those who turn from transgression.

basic.context: The chapter answers a real question: why has God's salvation not come? Not because His power failed, but because sin built a wall between the people and Him. After a devastatingly honest confession of what that sin actually looks like, lies, violence, no justice, God Himself steps in, finding no human able to intercede, and acts alone, armed for battle.
basic.hermeneutics: The confession in the middle of this chapter is corporate and unflinching, we all growl like bears, our transgressions testify against us, refusing any excuse. The turn in verse 16, God seeing no intercessor and acting Himself, is the theological hinge of the whole passage, salvation must originate entirely with God because no human mediator could be found sufficient.
basic.originalLanguages: God is amazed that there is no one to paga, intercede, so His own arm acts. And the goel, Redeemer, promised to come to Zion in verse 20 is quoted by Paul directly of Christ's future salvation for Israel, Romans 11:26.
basic.typology: God's own armor, righteousness as breastplate, salvation as helmet, anticipates Paul's armor of God in Ephesians 6:14-17, where believers are told to put on the very armor God wears here. The absence of a human intercessor anticipates and is answered by Christ, the one true Intercessor.
basic.christ: Paul quotes verse 20 directly in Romans 11:26, the Deliverer will come from Zion, applying it to Christ's coming salvation for Israel. And where this chapter says God found no one to intercede, Hebrews 7:25 answers that Christ always lives to make intercession for those who come to God through Him, the very lack this chapter mourns, filled.
basic.god: God does not excuse His people's sin, He names it in devastating detail, yet His justice does not leave Him passive; when no human mediator can be found, He Himself supplies both the righteousness and the salvation required, armed and moving toward Zion as Redeemer.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 11:26 quotes verse 20 directly of Christ's future salvation for Israel. Ephesians 6:14-17 has believers wear the same armor of righteousness and salvation God wears here. Hebrews 7:25 answers this chapter's missing intercessor with Christ, who always lives to intercede.
basic.oneStory: Sin always separates, and no human effort or mediator proves sufficient to close that gap; that is the honest diagnosis of this chapter. Its answer, God Himself acting, coming as armed Redeemer, is fulfilled in Christ, both the Deliverer Paul says will come from Zion and the great High Priest who intercedes exactly where this chapter found no one able to.

context.literary: Structured as courtroom confession, moving from a flat denial that God's arm is too short, verse 1, through an escalating, corporate confession of sin's true character, into a stunning divine soliloquy where God arms Himself for battle since no human intercessor exists, closing with a covenant promise of an enduring Spirit and word.
context.historical: Addressed to a community wrestling with delayed deliverance, tempted to blame God's power or attention; Isaiah insists the fault is entirely theirs, then reveals that only God's own initiative, not further human effort, can finally supply what justice requires.
context.canonical: Verse 20 becomes central to Paul's argument in Romans 11 about Israel's future salvation, and God's battle armor here becomes the template for the Christian's spiritual armor in Ephesians 6, linking this Old Testament oracle directly into New Testament theology of both salvation history and spiritual warfare.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct any suspicion that God's power or willingness had failed by locating the true barrier in the people's own sin, to lead them through honest, specific confession, and to reveal that God Himself, finding no adequate human intercessor, would act to bring redemption.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The extended confession, verses 3-15, functions almost liturgically, modeling the kind of specific, unflinching confession Israel, and every reader, is meant to make their own, not merely describing a past condition but prescribing the shape true repentance takes.

debates.question: Does Romans 11:26's use of Isaiah 59:20 teach a future national salvation for ethnic Israel?

views.label: Yes, a future turning of ethnic Israel to Christ
views.case: Paul's argument in Romans 11 anticipates a future period when hardened Israel as a nation will turn to Christ, fulfilling this verse in a yet-future, national sense.

views.label: Fulfilled progressively in the church, Jew and Gentile together
views.case: Some read Paul as describing the ongoing ingathering of a remnant of believing Jews alongside Gentiles into the one people of God, rather than a single future national event.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the same core truth this chapter teaches, that the Redeemer promised to Zion is Christ, and that His coming secures salvation for all, Jew and Gentile, who turn from transgression, whether that unfolds as an ongoing ingathering or a future decisive turning.

study.originalLanguages: God is astonished that there is no one to paga, intercede, on behalf of His people, so His own arm acts unaided. The goel, Redeemer, He promises to send to Zion in verse 20 is the same word used throughout Isaiah of God's kinsman-deliverer role, here quoted by Paul directly of Christ's coming to save.

typology.type: God's armor of righteousness and salvation, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: The Christian's armor of God, righteousness as breastplate and salvation as helmet, Ephesians 6:14, 17.

typology.type: No one found to intercede, verse 16
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who always lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through Him, Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The Redeemer who comes to Zion, verse 20
typology.fulfillment: Christ's coming salvation for Israel, quoted directly by Paul in Romans 11:26.

study.christ: Paul quotes verse 20 word for word in Romans 11:26 to describe Christ's coming as Deliverer from Zion, securing the future salvation of Israel. And where this chapter laments that God found no human intercessor able to bring justice, verse 16, the book of Hebrews answers that lack directly: Christ always lives to make intercession for those who come to God through Him, Hebrews 7:25. Paul even borrows this chapter's battle armor, righteousness as breastplate, salvation as helmet, and gives it to every believer as the armor of God, Ephesians 6:14-17, arming Christ's people with the very equipment God wears here.
study.god: God is neither weak nor inattentive; sin alone separates. When human intercession fails entirely, verse 16 shows God does not abandon justice, He supplies it Himself, arming His own righteousness and salvation to accomplish what no mediator on earth could achieve, and promising an enduring covenant of Spirit and word to those who turn from transgression.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Isaiah 59:20 directly of Christ's coming salvation for Israel.
Christ always lives to intercede, answering this chapter's lament that no intercessor could be found.
Believers wear the same armor of righteousness and salvation God arms Himself with here.
The same root for intercession describes the Servant bearing iniquity and interceding for transgressors.

study.oneStory: Sin builds a wall no ritual can tear down, and no human intercessor proves sufficient to close the gap between a guilty people and a holy God; that is this chapter's honest diagnosis. Its answer is God Himself, arming His own righteousness and salvation and coming as Redeemer to Zion, a promise Paul says is fulfilled in Christ, the intercessor this chapter could not find and the Deliverer this chapter promised.

quiz.q: According to verses 1-2, why has salvation seemed delayed?

options.text: The people's own iniquities have built a barrier between them and God, not any weakness in His arm or ear.
options.note: Verses 1-2 explicitly rule out divine weakness and name sin as the cause.

options.text: God's power had genuinely grown too weak to save.
God had forgotten His covenant with Israel.
options.note: This voices a real, felt fear addressed elsewhere in Isaiah, but the text here names sin as the barrier, not forgetfulness on God's part.

options.text: Because Israel had not offered enough sacrifices.
options.note: The chapter's diagnosis is moral, lies and violence, not a shortfall in ritual sacrifice.

quiz.q: What does God do when He finds no one to intercede, verses 16-17?

options.text: He acts Himself, arming His own righteousness like a breastplate and salvation like a helmet.
options.note: Verses 16-17 describe God supplying what no human mediator could.
The opposite; He acts decisively to bring salvation.

options.text: He waits indefinitely for a human intercessor to appear.
options.note: This is the opposite of what happens; He does not wait, He acts immediately Himself once none is found.

options.text: He commands the people to appoint a new priest.
options.note: The text describes God's own direct action, not an institutional appointment.

quiz.q: How does Paul use Isaiah 59:20 in Romans 11:26?

options.text: He quotes it directly of the Deliverer coming from Zion, applying it to Christ's coming salvation for Israel.
He applies it only to the historical return from Babylonian exile.
options.note: This is a real, minority reading of the underlying text, but Paul's argument in Romans 11 applies it to salvation through Christ, not merely the historical return from exile.

options.text: Paul never quotes Isaiah 59 in his letters.
He uses it to argue Israel is permanently excluded from salvation.
options.note: Paul's argument is the opposite, hope for Israel's future salvation.

quiz.q: What connection does Ephesians 6 draw with this chapter's armor imagery?

options.text: Believers are told to put on the same armor of righteousness and salvation God wears here, verses 17.
options.note: Ephesians 6:14, 17 draws directly on this armor language.

options.text: Ephesians describes entirely different armor with no relation to Isaiah.
options.note: The armor pieces named, righteousness and salvation, match directly.

options.text: It applies only to literal, physical warfare against nations.
options.note: This mistakes the armor's original battle imagery for its New Testament use; Ephesians 6 explicitly reframes it as spiritual warfare, not combat between nations.

options.text: It is used to argue Christians should avoid all conflict.
options.note: The armor imagery equips believers for spiritual struggle, not passive avoidance of all conflict.

questions: The chapter says sin, not God's weakness, is what separates. What sin have you been quietly blaming on distance from God rather than owning honestly?
The confession in verses 3-15 is specific and unflinching, naming lies, violence, and injustice by name. Is your own confession this specific, or does it stay vague and general?
God was amazed that there was no one to intercede for justice. Is there a situation around you crying out for someone to stand in the gap, and could that someone be you?
God armed Himself with righteousness and salvation because no human mediator could be found. How does knowing Christ now intercedes for you change how you approach your own failures?

The chapter closes with a promise that God's Spirit and words will never depart from His people's mouths, even their children's children. What would it look like to actively pass on God's word to the next generation in your own family?

translationNotes.note: Where the sun sets, and His glory where it rises describes east to west, a merism for the whole earth fearing the LORD's name, not a claim about two separate divine glories.

Paul's quotation in Romans 11:26 follows the Greek Septuagint's rendering, the Deliverer will come out of Zion, slightly different in preposition from the Hebrew's to Zion, though the theological sense, salvation coming from and reaching Zion, is preserved either way.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 59:1-21; no em dashes present in this passage.
